raw_question_text: A d.c. electromagnet is to be constructed by winding a coil of $N$ turns tightly on an iron yoke shaped like a doughnut with a small slab sliced out to form the gap as in Fig. 2.34. The radii for the doughnut are $a$ and $b$ and the width of the gap is $W$. The permeability $\mu$ for the iron can be assumed constant and large. A wire of radius $r$ and resistivity $\rho$ is to be used for the coil. The completed magnet will be operated by placing the coil across a d.c. power supply of voltage $V$. For simplicity, assume that $b/a \gg 1$ and $a/r \gg 1$. Derive expressions for the following quantities: The steady state value for the magnetic field in the gap.
normalized_question_text: A d.c. electromagnet is to be constructed by winding a coil of $N$ turns tightly on an iron yoke shaped like a doughnut with a small slab sliced out to form the gap as in Fig. 2.34. The radii for the doughnut are $a$ and $b$ and the width of the gap is $W$. The permeability $\mu$ for the iron can be assumed constant and large. A wire of radius $r$ and resistivity $\rho$ is to be used for the coil. The completed magnet will be operated by placing the coil across a d.c. power supply of voltage $V$. For simplicity, assume that $b/a \gg 1$ and $a/r \gg 1$. Derive expressions for the following quantities: the steady state value for the magnetic field in the gap.
reasoning_chain: In the steady state, as $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B}=0$ and the cross section of the iron yoke is the same everywhere, $B$ must be a constant in the yoke. Applying $\oint \mathbf{H} \cdot d \mathbf{l}=NI$ to the doughnut, we have $\frac{B}{\mu}(2\pi b-W)+\frac{B}{\mu_{0}}W=NI$ giving $B=\frac{NI\mu_{0}\mu}{\mu_{0}(2\pi b-W)+\mu W}\approx\frac{NI\mu_{0}\mu}{\mu_{0}2\pi b+\mu W}$. As $I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{V}{\rho\frac{N2\pi a}{\pi r^{2}}}=\frac{Vr^{2}}{2a\rho N}$, the steady state value for the magnetic field in the gap is $B=\frac{\mu_{0}\mu V r^{2}}{2a\rho(2\pi b\mu_{0}+W\mu)}$.

A sphere of radius $a$ ($a \ll R$) made of paramagnetic material with permeability $\mu$ is placed with its center at the center of the circuit. Determine the magnetic dipole moment of the sphere resulting from the magnetic field of the current.

raw_question_text: A circular wire of radius $R$ carries a current $i$ electromagnetic units. A sphere of radius $a$ ($a \ll R$) made of paramagnetic material with permeability $\mu$ is placed with its center at the center of the circuit. Determine the force per unit area on the sphere.
normalized_question_text: A circular wire of radius $R$ carries a current $i$ electromagnetic units. A sphere of radius $a$ ($a \ll R$) made of paramagnetic material with permeability $\mu$ is placed with its center at the center of the circuit. Determine the force per unit area on the sphere.
reasoning_chain: The surface current density on the sphere is given by the boundary condition $\alpha_{m}=\mathbf{n}\times(\mathbf{H}_{2}-\mathbf{H}_{1})$. With $\mathbf{H}_{1}=\frac{\mathbf{B}_{1}}{\mu_{0}}$ and $\mathbf{H}_{2}=\frac{\mathbf{B}_{2}}{\mu}-\mathbf{M}$, and using the continuity condition $\mathbf{n}\times(\mathbf{B}_{2}-\mathbf{B}_{1})=0$, one finds $\alpha_{M}=\mathbf{M}\times\mathbf{n}=\frac{3(\mu-\mu_{0}) i}{2(\mu+2\mu_{0})R}\sin\theta\,\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}$. Then the force per unit area on the sphere is obtained by $\mathbf{f}=\alpha_{M}\times\mathbf{B}_{0}$. Substituting $\mathbf{B}_{0}=\frac{\mu_{0} i}{2R}\mathbf{e}_{z}$ and evaluating the cross product, we arrive at $\mathbf{f}=\frac{3\mu_{0}(\mu-\mu_{0}) i^{2}}{4(\mu+2\mu_{0})R}\sin\theta\left(\cos\theta\,\mathbf{e}_{\theta}+\sin\theta\,\mathbf{e}_{r}\right)$.

raw_question_text: In Fig. 2.56 an iron needle 1 cm long and 0.1 cm in diameter is placed in a uniform magnetic field of $H_{0}=1000 \mathrm{Gs}$ with its long axis along the field direction. Give an approximate formula for $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{r})$ valid for distances $r \gg 1 \mathrm{~cm}$. Here $r$ is measured from the center of the needle as origin. (Note: the saturation value of $\mathbf{B}$ in iron is approximately 2000 Gs.)
normalized_question_text: In Fig. 2.56 an iron needle 1 cm long and 0.1 cm in diameter is placed in a uniform magnetic field of $H_{0}=1000 \mathrm{Gs}$ with its long axis along the field direction. Give an approximate formula for $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{r})$ valid for distances $r \gg 1 \mathrm{~cm}$. Here $r$ is measured from the center of the needle as origin. (Note: the saturation value of $\mathbf{B}$ in iron is approximately 2000 Gs.)
reasoning_chain: For distances $r \gg 1 \mathrm{~cm}$ the iron needle can be treated as a magnetic dipole with moment $\mathbf{m}$. Take the $z$-axis along the axis of the needle. Write $\mathbf{H}_{\text{ext}}=H_{0}\mathbf{e}_{z}$, $\mathbf{m}=m\mathbf{e}_{z}$. In Gaussian units the magnetic field at position $\mathbf{r}$ is approximately $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{r})=\mathbf{H}_{0}-\nabla\left(\frac{\mathbf{m}\cdot\mathbf{r}}{r^{3}}\right)=\mathbf{H}_{0}-\frac{\mathbf{m}}{r^{3}}+\frac{3(\mathbf{m}\cdot\mathbf{r})\mathbf{r}}{r^{5}}$. As the magnetic field due to the iron needle is much weaker than the external field $H_{0}$, $m/r^{3}\ll H_{0}$. As the tangential component of $\mathbf{H}$ is continuous at the boundary, the magnetic field within the iron needle may be taken as approximately $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{in}}=\mathbf{H}_{0}$. The magnetization of the needle is then $\mathbf{M}=\frac{1}{4\pi}\left(\mathbf{B}_{\text{in}}-\mathbf{H}_{\text{in}}\right)$. With the volume of the needle equal to $V=(0.05)^{2}\pi\,\mathrm{cm}^{3}$, $B_{\text{in}}=2\times10^{4}\mathrm{Gs}$, $H_{\text{in}}\approx H_{0}=10^{3}\mathrm{Gs}$, the magnetic moment of the needle is $m=V\,M=\frac{(0.05)^{2}\pi}{4\pi}\times(2-0.1)\times10^{4}=11.9\,\mathrm{Gs}\cdot\mathrm{cm}^{3}$. In polar coordinates the magnetic field at distances $r \gg 1 \mathrm{~cm}$ has components $H_{r}=H_{0}\cos\theta+2m\frac{\cos\theta}{r^{3}}=(1000+\frac{23.8}{r^{3}})\cos\theta\,\mathrm{Gs}$ and $H_{\theta}=-H_{0}\cos\theta+\frac{m\sin\theta}{r^{3}}=(-1000+\frac{11.9}{r^{3}})\sin\theta\,\mathrm{Gs}$.

raw_question_text: A long solenoid of radius $b$ and length $l$ is wound so that the axial magnetic field is $$\mathbf{B}=\begin{cases} B_{0}\,\mathbf{e}_{z}, & r<b,\\ 0, & r>b, \end{cases}$$ A particle of charge $q$ is emitted with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a central rod of radius $a$ (see Fig. 2.59). The electric force on the particle is given by $qE=f(r)\,\mathbf{e}_{r}$, where $\mathbf{e}_{r}\cdot\mathbf{e}_{z}=0$. We assume $\mathbf{v}$ is sufficiently large so that the particle passes out of the solenoid and does so without hitting the solenoid. Find the angular momentum of the particle about the axis of the solenoid, for $r>b$.
normalized_question_text: A long solenoid of radius $b$ and length $l$ is wound so that the axial magnetic field is $$\mathbf{B}=\begin{cases} B_{0}\,\mathbf{e}_{z}, & r<b,\\ 0, & r>b, \end{cases}$$ A particle of charge $q$ is emitted with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a central rod of radius $a$ (see Fig. 2.59). The electric force on the particle is given by $qE=f(r)\,\mathbf{e}_{r}$, where $\mathbf{e}_{r}\cdot\mathbf{e}_{z}=0$. We assume $\mathbf{v}$ is sufficiently large so that the particle passes out of the solenoid and does so without hitting the solenoid. Find the angular momentum of the particle about the axis of the solenoid, for $r>b$.
reasoning_chain: As $\mathbf{v}$ is very large, we can consider any deviation from a straight line path to be quite small in the emitting process. Let $v_{\perp}$ be the transverse velocity of the particle. We have $m\frac{d\mathbf{v}_{\perp}}{dt}=q\,\mathbf{v}\times\mathbf{B}$. As $\mathbf{v}\perp\mathbf{B}$, $dv_{\perp}=\frac{q}{m}B_{0}vd t=\frac{q}{m}B_{0}dr$ and $$v_{\perp}(b)=\int_{a}^{b}\frac{q}{m}B_{0}\,dr=\frac{q}{m}B_{0}(b-a).$$ At $r=b$ the angular momentum of the particle about the axis of the solenoid has magnitude $$|\mathbf{r}\times m\mathbf{v}_{\perp}(b)|_{r=b}=mb\,v_{\perp}(b)=qB_{0}b(b-a)$$ and direction $-\mathbf{e}_{z}$. Thus the angular momentum is $$\mathbf{J}_{p}=-qB_{0}b(b-a)\,\mathbf{e}_{z}.$$ For $r>b$, $\mathbf{B}=0$ and $\mathbf{J}_{p}$ is considered. So $\mathbf{J}_{p}$ is the angular momentum of the particle about the axis for $r>b$.

raw_question_text: Figure 2.75 shows two long parallel wires carrying equal and opposite steady currents $I$ and separated by a distance $2a$. Consult the diagram 2.76 which gives specific dimensions and which represents two long pipes of circular cross section carrying equal and opposite charges $q$ (per cm). Given that the field is $E=8000\,\mathrm{V/cm}$ at the position $z=a=0.5\,\mathrm{cm}$, calculate the value of $q$ and the potential difference between the two pipes.
normalized_question_text: Figure 2.75 shows two long parallel wires carrying equal and opposite steady currents $I$ and separated by a distance $2a$. Consult diagram 2.76, which gives specific dimensions and represents two long pipes of circular cross section carrying equal and opposite charges $q$ (per cm). Given that the field is $E=8000\,\mathrm{V/cm}$ at the position $z=a=0.5\,\mathrm{cm}$, calculate the value of $q$ and the potential difference between the two pipes.
reasoning_chain: By analogy, Eq. (1) gives $E_{z}=-\frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{a}{(z^2+a^2)}$. With $a=z=5\times10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m}$, $E_{z}=8\times10^{5}\,\mathrm{V/m}$, we have $q=8.90\times10^{-7}\,\mathrm{C}$. The potential difference between the two cylindrical sides carrying opposite charges $q$ per unit length is $\Delta V=\frac{qa}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\int_{z_{1}}^{z_{2}}\frac{dz}{z^2+a^2}=\left.\frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\arctan\left(\frac{z}{a}\right)\right|_{z_{1}}^{z_{2}}=2.7\times10^{3}\,\mathrm{V}$, with $z_{1}=4\times10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m}$ and $z_{2}=8\times10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m}$.

raw_question_text: A Hall probe with dimensions as shown in Fig. 2.81 has conductivity $\sigma$ and carries charge density $\rho$. The probe is placed in an unknown magnetic field $B$ oriented along the $+y$ direction. An external potential $V_{\text {ext }}$ is applied to two ends producing an electric field in the $+z$ direction. Derive an expression for $B$ in terms of $V_{\text {Hall }}, V_{\text {ext }}, \sigma, \rho$ and the dimensions of the probe.
normalized_question_text: A Hall probe with dimensions as shown in Fig. 2.81 has conductivity $\sigma$ and carries charge density $\rho$. The probe is placed in an unknown magnetic field $B$ oriented along the $+y$ direction. An external potential $V_{\text{ext}}$ is applied to two ends, producing an electric field in the $+z$ direction. Derive an expression for $B$ in terms of $V_{\text{Hall}}, V_{\text{ext}}, \sigma, \rho$ and the dimensions of the probe.
reasoning_chain: For equilibrium we have $qE_{\text {Hall }}=qBv$. As $E_{\text {Hall }}=\frac{V_{\text {Hall }}}{h}$ and $v=\frac{j}{\rho}=\frac{\sigma E_{\text {ext }}}{\rho}=\frac{\sigma}{\rho}\cdot\frac{V_{\text {ext }}}{l}$, the above gives $\frac{V_{\text {Hall }}}{h}=B\frac{\sigma}{\rho}\cdot\frac{V_{\text {ext }}}{l}$, or $B=\frac{V_{\text {Hall }}}{V_{\text {ext }}}\cdot\frac{\rho l}{\sigma h}$.

raw_question_text: A model for an electron consists of a shell of charge distributed uniformly on the surface of a sphere of radius $a$. The electron moves with velocity $v\ll c$. Use the value of $a$ to calculate the energy in the field of the moving charge and compare it with the rest-mass energy and kinetic energy.
normalized_question_text: A model for an electron consists of a shell of charge distributed uniformly on the surface of a sphere of radius $a$. The electron moves with velocity $v\ll c$. Use the value of $a$ to calculate the energy in the field of the moving charge and compare it with the rest-mass energy and kinetic energy.
reasoning_chain: The energy in the electromagnetic field is obtained from $W=\int \frac{1}{8\pi}(E^2+B^2)dV$. Inserting $E=\frac{e}{r^2}$ and $B=\frac{ev\sin\theta}{cr^2}$, and integrating over space (with $r$ from $a$ to $\infty$ and over angular variables), one finds $W=\frac{e^2}{2a}\left(1+\frac{2}{3}\frac{v^2}{c^2}\right)$. Recognizing that $\frac{e^2}{2a}=\frac{3}{4}mc^2$, this result can be written as $W=\frac{3}{4}mc^2+\frac{1}{2}mv^2$, so that for $v\ll c$, the kinetic energy contribution is much smaller than the rest energy.

raw_question_text: In the electrical circuit shown in Fig. 3.36, $\omega$, $R_{1}$, $R_{2}$ and $L$ are fixed; $C$ and $M$ (the mutual inductance between the identical inductors $L$) can be varied. Find values of $M$ and $C$ which maximize the power dissipated in resistor $R_{2}$. What is the maximum power? You may assume, if needed, $R_{2}>R_{1}, \omega L / R_{2}>10$.
normalized_question_text: In the electrical circuit shown in Fig. 3.36, $\omega$, $R_{1}$, $R_{2}$ and $L$ are fixed; $C$ and $M$ (the mutual inductance between the identical inductors $L$) can be varied. Find values of $M$ and $C$ which maximize the power dissipated in resistor $R_{2}$. What is the maximum power? You may assume, if needed, $R_{2}>R_{1}, \omega L / R_{2}>10$.
reasoning_chain: Assuming that the primary and secondary currents are directed as in Fig. 3.36, we have the circuit equations $\dot{V}_{0} = R_{1}\dot{I}_{1} + \dot{I}_{1}\left[\frac{1}{j\omega C} + j\omega L\right] + j\omega M\dot{I}_{2}$ and $0 = \dot{I}_{2}R_{2} + j\omega L\dot{I}_{2} + j\omega M\dot{I}_{1}$. The above simultaneous equations have solution $I_{2} = \frac{j\omega M C V_{0}}{C\left[\omega^{2}(L^{2}-M^{2}) - R_{1}R_{2}\right] - L + j\left[\frac{R_{2}}{\omega} - \omega L C(R_{1}+R_{2})\right]}$. As $P_{2} = \frac{1}{2}|I_{2}|^{2}R_{2}$, when $|I_{2}|$ is maximized $P_{2}$ is maximized also. We have $|I_{2}| = \frac{\omega V_{0}}{\left\{\left[\frac{1}{M}\left(\omega^{2}L^{2} - R_{1}R_{2} - \frac{L}{C}\right) - \omega^{2}M\right]^{2} + \left[\frac{1}{MC}\left(\frac{R_{2}}{\omega} - \omega L C(R_{1}+R_{2})\right)\right]^{2}\right\}^{\frac{3}{2}}}$. As the numerator is fixed and the denominator is the square root of the sum of two squared terms, when the two squared terms are minimum at the same time $|I_{2}|$ will achieve its maximum. The minimum of the second squared term is zero, for which we require $C = \frac{R_{2}}{\omega^{2}L(R_{1}+R_{2})}$, giving $|I_{2}| = \frac{\omega V_{0}}{\omega^{2}M + \frac{R_{1}R_{2} + \frac{R_{1}\omega^{2}L^{2}}{R_{2}}}{M}}$. Minimizing the above denominator, we require $M = \sqrt{\frac{R_{1}R_{2}}{\omega^{2}} + \frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}L^{2}}$. Hence, for $M = \sqrt{\frac{R_{1}R_{2}}{\omega^{2}} + \frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}L^{2}}$, $C = \frac{R_{2}}{\omega^{2}L(R_{1}+R_{2})}$, $P_{2}$ is maximum, having the value $P_{2} = \frac{1}{2}|I_{2}|^{2}R_{2} = \frac{V_{0}^{2}}{8R_{1}\left(1+\frac{\omega^{2}L^{2}}{R_{2}^{2}}\right)}$. Supposing $\omega L / R_{2} > 10$, we obtain $P_{2} = \frac{V_{0}^{2}R_{2}^{2}}{8\omega^{2}L^{2}R_{1}}$ as the maximum power dissipated in $R_{2}$.

The coil $L$ as shown is initially in a magnetic field $B_{0}=0$. Switch $S$ is then closed, current $I=1 \mathrm{amp}$ flows, and $G$ deflects $\theta_{1}=0.5$ radian and returns to rest. Then the coil is quickly moved into a magnetic field $B_{2}$, and $G$ is observed to deflect $\theta_{2}=1$ radian. What is the field $B_{2}$ (in any specified units)?
reasoning_chain:
Solution:
The direction of $\mathbf{B}_{2}$ is illustrated in Fig. 3.47.
Let the self-inductance of the coil $L$ be $L_{1}$, then
$$
\varepsilon_{1}=M  rac{d i_{2}}{d t}+L_{1}  rac{d i_{1}}{d t}
$$
or
$$
 rac{d q}{d t}=i_{1}= rac{\varepsilon_{1}}{R_{1}}= rac{M}{R_{1}}  rac{d i_{2}}{d t}+ rac{L_{1}}{R_{1}}  rac{d i_{1}}{d t},
$$
with
$$
i_{2}(0)=0, \quad i_{2}(\infty)=1 \mathrm{~A}, \quad i_{1}(0)=i_{1}(\infty)=0
$$

Integrating the circuit equation we obtain
$$
q_{1}=\int_{0}^{\infty}  rac{M}{R_{1}} d i_{2}+\int_{0}^{\infty}  rac{L_{1}}{R_{1}} d i_{1}= rac{M}{R_{1}} i_{2}(\infty) .
$$

When the coil is moved into the magnetic field $\mathbf{B}_{2}$, its induced emf is
$$
\varepsilon_{2}=- rac{d \psi}{d t}
$$
with
$$
\psi_{1}(\infty)=-N B_{2} S, \quad \psi_{1}(0)=0 .
$$

Thus
$$
 rac{d q_{2}}{d t}=i_{2}= rac{\varepsilon_{2}}{R_{1}}= rac{-1}{R_{1}}  rac{d \psi_{1}}{d t}
$$
giving
$$
q_{2}= rac{N B_{2} \pi a^{2}}{R_{1}} .
$$

As $q \propto 	heta$, we have
$$
 rac{	heta_{1}}{	heta_{2}}= rac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}= rac{M i_{2}(\infty)}{N B_{2} \pi a^{2}},
$$
or
$$
B_{2}= rac{	heta_{2} M i_{2}(\infty)}{	heta_{1} N \pi a^{2}}= rac{1 	imes 1 	imes 1}{0.5 	imes 100 	imes \pi 	imes 10^{-4}}=63.4 \mathrm{~T} .
$$

raw_question_text: The square-box in Fig. 3.71 represents an unknown linear lumpedconstant passive network. The source of emf at the left is assumed to have zero internal impedance. It is known that if the input emf $e_{i}(t)$ is a step function, i.e., $e_{i}(t)=\begin{cases}0 & t\leq 0\\ A & t>0\end{cases}$ then the open-circuit (no-load) output voltage $e_{0}(t)$ is of the form $e_{0}(t)=\begin{cases}0, & t\leq 0\\ \frac{1}{2}A[1-\exp(-t/\tau)], & t>0\end{cases}$ where the constant $\tau$ has the value $\tau=1.2\times10^{-4} \;\mathrm{s}$. Find the open-circuit (no load) output voltage $e_{0}(t)$ when the input is given by $e_{i}(t)=4\cos(\omega t)$ volts where $\omega$ corresponds to the frequency 1500 cycles/sec.
normalized_question_text: The square box in Fig. 3.71 represents an unknown linear lumped-constant passive network. The source of emf at the left is assumed to have zero internal impedance. It is known that if the input emf $e_i(t)$ is a step function, i.e., $e_i(t)=\begin{cases}0 & t\leq 0\\ A & t>0\end{cases}$, then the open-circuit (no-load) output voltage $e_0(t)$ is of the form $e_0(t)=\begin{cases}0, & t\leq 0\\ \frac{1}{2}A[1-\exp(-t/\tau)], & t>0\end{cases}$, where the constant $\tau$ has the value $\tau=1.2\times10^{-4}\,\mathrm{s}$. Find the open-circuit (no-load) output voltage $e_0(t)$ when the input is given by $e_i(t)=4\cos(\omega t)$ volts, where $\omega$ corresponds to the frequency 1500 cycles/sec.
reasoning_chain: We first use the Laplace transform to find the transmission function $H(s)$ of the network in the frequency domain. The Laplace transform of the equation $e_{i}(t)=A \cdot U(t)$ is $E_{i}(s)=A/S$. Similarly, the Laplace transform of the output $e_{0}(t)$ is $E_{0}(s)=\frac{1}{2}A\left[\frac{1}{S}-\frac{1}{S+1/\tau}\right]$. Hence the transmission function is $H(s)=\frac{E_{0}(s)}{E_{i}(s)}=\frac{\frac{1}{2\tau}}{s+\frac{1}{\tau}}$. The Laplace transform of the new input $e_{i}(t)=4\cos(\omega t)$ is $E_{i}(s)=\frac{4s}{\omega^{2}+s^{2}}$ giving the output as $E_{0}(s)=E_{i}(s)\cdot H(s)=\frac{4s}{\omega^{2}+s^{2}}\times\frac{\frac{1}{2\tau}}{s+\frac{1}{\tau}}=\frac{2}{\tau}\left[\frac{1}{s+\frac{1}{\tau}}\cdot\frac{-\frac{1}{\tau}}{\omega^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right)^{2}}+\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{s-i\omega}\times\frac{1}{i\omega+\frac{1}{\tau}}+\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{s+i\omega}\times\frac{1}{i\omega+\frac{1}{\tau}}\right]$, where $\omega\tau=2\pi\times1500\times1.2\times10^{-4}\approx1$. The reverse transformation of $E_{0}(s)$ gives the open-circuit output voltage $V_{0}(t)=\frac{2}{\tau}\left[\frac{-\frac{1}{\tau}}{\omega^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right)^{2}}e^{-t/\tau}+\frac{\frac{1}{\tau}\cos(\omega t)+\omega\sin(\omega t)}{\omega^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right)^{2}}\right]\simeq-e^{-t/\tau}+\cos(\omega t)+\sin(\omega t)$

raw_question_text: The pions that are produced when protons strike the target at Fermilab are not all moving parallel to the initial proton beam. A focusing device, called a "horn", (actually two of them are used as a pair) is used to deflect the pions so as to cause them to move more closely towards the proton beam direction. This device consists of an inner cylindrical conductor along which a current flows in one direction and an outer cylindrical conductor along which the current returns. Between these two surfaces there is produced a toroidal magnetic field that deflects the mesons that pass through this region. At first, calculate the approximate inductance of this horn using the dimensions shown in the figure. The current of charged pions and protons is negligible compared with the current in the conductors.
normalized_question_text: The pions that are produced when protons strike the target at Fermilab are not all moving parallel to the initial proton beam. A focusing device, called a "horn" (actually two of them are used as a pair), is used to deflect the pions so as to cause them to move more closely toward the proton beam direction. This device consists of an inner cylindrical conductor along which a current flows in one direction and an outer cylindrical conductor along which the current returns. Between these two surfaces there is produced a toroidal magnetic field that deflects the mesons that pass through this region. Calculate the approximate inductance of this horn using the dimensions shown in the figure. The current of charged pions and protons is negligible compared with the current in the conductors.
reasoning_chain: The magnetic induction at a point between the cylinders distance $r$ from the axis is in the $\mathbf{e}_{\theta}$ direction and has magnitude $B=\frac{\mu_{0}I}{2\pi r}$. The magnetic flux crossing a longitudinal cross section of a unit length of the horn is $\phi=2\int_{0.05}^{0.4}B\,dr \approx \frac{\mu_{0}I}{\pi}\int_{0.05}^{0.4}\frac{1}{r}\,dr = 8.3\times10^{-7}\cdot I$. Hence the inductance is approximately $L=\frac{\phi}{I} \approx 8.3\times10^{-7}\,\mathrm{H}$.

raw_question_text: The pions that are produced when protons strike the target at Fermilab are not all moving parallel to the initial proton beam. A focusing device, called a "horn", (actually two of them are used as a pair) is used to deflect the pions so as to cause them to move more closely towards the proton beam direction. This device consists of an inner cylindrical conductor along which a current flows in one direction and an outer cylindrical conductor along which the current returns. Between these two surfaces there is produced a toroidal magnetic field that deflects the mesons that pass through this region. The current is provided by a capacitor bank ($C=2400\,\mu\mathrm{F}$), that is discharged (at an appropriate time before the pulse of protons strikes the target) into a transmission line that connects the two horns. The total inductance of both horns and the transmission line is $3.8\times10^{-6}$ henries as in Fig. 3.78(b). In the circuit the charged voltage of the capacitor is $V_{0}=14\,\mathrm{kV}$ and the resistance is $R=8.5\times10^{-3}$ ohm. How many seconds after the switch is thrown does it take for the current to reach its maximum value?
normalized_question_text: The pions that are produced when protons strike the target at Fermilab are not all moving parallel to the initial proton beam. A focusing device, called a "horn", (actually two of them are used as a pair) is used to deflect the pions so as to cause them to move more closely towards the proton beam direction. This device consists of an inner cylindrical conductor along which a current flows in one direction and an outer cylindrical conductor along which the current returns. Between these two surfaces there is produced a toroidal magnetic field that deflects the mesons that pass through this region. The current is provided by a capacitor bank ($C=2400\,\mu\mathrm{F}$), that is discharged (at an appropriate time before the pulse of protons strikes the target) into a transmission line that connects the two horns. The total inductance of both horns and the transmission line is $3.8\times10^{-6}$ henries as in Fig. 3.78(b). In the circuit the charged voltage of the capacitor is $V_{0}=14\,\mathrm{kV}$ and the resistance is $R=8.5\times10^{-3}$ ohm. How many seconds after the switch is thrown does it take for the current to reach its maximum value?
reasoning_chain: Let the current of the RCL loop be $i(t)$. We have $u_{C}+u_{L}+u_{R}=0$ with $I=C\frac{du_{C}}{dt}$, $u_{R}=RC\frac{du_{C}}{dt}$, and $u_{L}=LC\frac{d^{2}u_{C}}{dt^{2}}$, i.e., $LC\frac{d^{2}u_{C}}{dt^{2}}+RC\frac{du_{C}}{dt}+u_{C}=0$ with the initial condition $u_{C}(0)=V_{0}$. Assuming a solution of the form $u_{C}=u_{0}e^{-i\omega t}$ leads to $\omega=-i\alpha\pm\omega_{d}$ with $\omega_{d}=\sqrt{\omega_{0}^{2}-\alpha^{2}}$, where $\omega_{0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ and $\alpha=\frac{R}{2L}$. With $\alpha=1.118\times10^{3}\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ and $\omega_{0}=1.047\times10^{5}\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ (so that $\omega_{0}\gg\alpha$ and $\omega_{d}\approx\omega_{0}$), the current in the loop is $I(t)\approx -C\omega_{0}V_{0}e^{-\alpha t}\sin(\omega_{0}t) = -3.52\times10^{6}e^{-1118t}\sin(1.047\times10^{5}t)$. Setting $\frac{dI(t)}{dt}=0$ yields the maximum when $t=\frac{1}{\alpha}=8.94\times10^{-4}\,\mathrm{s}$.

raw_question_text: Consider the situation shown in Fig. 4.28 where a perfectly conducting thin wire connects two small metallic balls. Suppose the charge density is given by $\rho(\mathbf{x}, t)=[\delta(z-a)-\delta(z+a)]\delta(x)\delta(y) Q\cos(\omega_{0}t)$. The current flows between the metallic balls through the thin wire. $a$, $Q$, and $\omega_{0}$ are constants. Calculate $\frac{d\bar{P}}{d\Omega}$, the average power emitted per unit solid angle in the dipole approximation.
normalized_question_text: Consider the situation shown in Fig. 4.28 where a perfectly conducting thin wire connects two small metallic balls. Suppose the charge density is given by $\rho(\mathbf{x}, t)=[\delta(z-a)-\delta(z+a)]\delta(x)\delta(y) Q\cos(\omega_{0}t)$. The current flows between the metallic balls through the thin wire. $a$, $Q$, and $\omega_{0}$ are constants. Calculate $\frac{d\bar{P}}{d\Omega}$, the average power emitted per unit solid angle in the dipole approximation.
reasoning_chain: The moment of the dipole is $\mathbf{p}=2Qa\cos(\omega_{0}t)\mathbf{e}_{z}$, or the real part of $2Qae^{-i\omega_{0}t}\mathbf{e}_{z}$. The average power per unit solid angle at $\mathbf{R}$ is $$\frac{d\bar{P}}{d\Omega}=\frac{|\ddot{\mathbf{p}}|^{2}}{32\pi^{2}\varepsilon_{0}c^{3}}\sin^{2}\theta=\frac{Q^{2}a^{2}\omega_{0}^{4}\sin^{2}\theta}{8\pi^{2}\varepsilon_{0}c^{3}}.$$

raw_question_text: Consider the situation shown in Fig. 4.28 where a perfectly conducting thin wire connects two small metallic balls. Suppose the charge density is given by $\rho(\mathbf{x}, t)=[\delta(z-a)-\delta(z+a)]\delta(x)\delta(y) Q\cos(\omega_{0}t)$. The current flows between the metallic balls through the thin wire. $a$, $Q$, and $\omega_{0}$ are constants. Calculate $\frac{d\bar{P}}{d\Omega}$ exactly.
normalized_question_text: Consider the situation shown in Fig. 4.28 where a perfectly conducting thin wire connects two small metallic balls. Suppose the charge density is given by $\rho(\mathbf{x}, t)=[\delta(z-a)-\delta(z+a)]\delta(x)\delta(y) Q\cos(\omega_{0}t)$. The current flows between the metallic balls through the thin wire. $a$, $Q$, and $\omega_{0}$ are constants. Calculate $\frac{d\bar{P}}{d\Omega}$ exactly.
reasoning_chain: A current flows through the wire connecting the two small metallic balls with density $$\mathbf{j}(\mathbf{x},t)=\mathbf{e}_{x}\frac{d}{dt}\int\rho\,dz=-i\omega_{0}Q\delta(x)\delta(y)e^{-i\omega_{0}t}\mathbf{e}_{z},\quad |z|\le a,$$ and produces a vector potential $$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{x},t)=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4\pi}\int_{V}\frac{\mathbf{j}(\mathbf{x}',t')}{r}dV',$$ where $t'=t-\frac{r}{c}$ and $r=|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}'|$. Thus, $$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{x},t)=-i\frac{\mu_{0}\omega_{0}Qe^{-i\omega_{0}t}}{4\pi}\int_{-a}^{a}\frac{e^{ik_{0}r}}{r}\,dz'\mathbf{e}_{z},$$ with $k_{0}=\frac{\omega_{0}}{c}$. Let $R=|x|$, so that $r^{2}=R^{2}-2Rz'\cos\theta+z'^{2}$. Hence, $$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{x},t)=-i\frac{\mu_{0}\omega_{0}Qe^{-i\omega_{0}t}}{4\pi}\int_{-a}^{a}\frac{e^{ik_{0}\sqrt{R^{2}+z'^{2}-2Rz'\cos\theta}}}{\sqrt{R^{2}+z'^{2}-2Rz'\cos\theta}}dz'\mathbf{e}_{z}.$$ For $R\gg a$, using the approximations $\frac{1}{r}\approx\frac{1}{R}$ and $\sqrt{R^{2}+z'^{2}-2Rz'\cos\theta}\approx R-z'\cos\theta$, we obtain $$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{x},t)=-i\frac{\mu_{0}\omega_{0}Qe^{i(k_{0}R-\omega_{0}t)}}{4\pi R}\int_{-a}^{a}e^{-ik_{0}z'\cos\theta}dz'\mathbf{e}_{z}=-\frac{iQe^{i(k_{0}R-\omega_{0}t)}}{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}cR}\frac{\sin(k_{0}a\cos\theta)}{\cos\theta}\mathbf{e}_{z}.$$ In spherical coordinates, with $\mathbf{e}_{z}=\cos\theta\mathbf{e}_{R}-\sin\theta\mathbf{e}_{\theta}$, the magnetic field is calculated as $$\mathbf{B}=\nabla\times\mathbf{A}\approx -\frac{k_{0}Qe^{i(k_{0}R-\omega_{0}t)}}{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}cR}\frac{\sin(k_{0)a\cos\theta)\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}.$$ The time-averaged Poynting vector becomes $$\overline{\mathbf{S}}=\frac{c}{2\mu_{0}}|\mathbf{B}|^{2}\mathbf{e}_{R}=\frac{\omega_{0}^{2}Q^{2}}{8\pi\varepsilon_{0}cR^{2}}\frac{\sin^{2}(k_{0}a\cos\theta)\sin^{2}\theta}{\cos^{2}\theta}\mathbf{e}_{R},$$ so that $$\frac{d\bar{P}}{d\Omega}=\frac{\omega_{0}^{2}Q^{2}}{8\pi^{2}\varepsilon_{0}c}\frac{\sin^{2}\theta\sin^{2}(k_{0}a\cos\theta)}{\cos^{2}\theta}.$$

raw_question_text: Two electric dipole oscillators vibrate with the same frequency $\omega$, but their phases differ by $\frac{\pi}{2}$. The amplitudes of the dipole moments are both equal to $\mathbf{P}_{0}$, but the two vectors are at an angle $\psi_{0}$ to each other, (let $\mathbf{P}_{1}$ be along the $x$-axis and $\mathbf{P}_{2}$ in the $xy$ plane) as in Fig. 4.36. For an oscillating dipole $\mathbf{P}$ at the origin, the $\mathbf{B}$-field in the radiation zone is given by $\mathbf{B}=k^{2} \frac{1}{r} e^{i k r}\left(\frac{\mathbf{r}}{r} \times \mathbf{P}\right)$. Find the average angular distribution.
normalized_question_text: Two electric dipole oscillators vibrate with the same frequency $\omega$, but their phases differ by $\frac{\pi}{2}$. The amplitudes of the dipole moments are both equal to $\mathbf{P}_{0}$, and the two vectors are at an angle $\psi_{0}$ to each other. Let $\mathbf{P}_{1}$ be along the $x$-axis and $\mathbf{P}_{2}$ lie in the $xy$ plane. For an oscillating dipole $\mathbf{P}$ at the origin, the $\mathbf{B}$-field in the radiation zone is given by $\mathbf{B}=k^{2} \frac{1}{r} e^{i k r}\left(\frac{\mathbf{r}}{r} \times \mathbf{P}\right)$. Find the average angular distribution.
reasoning_chain: The electric dipole moments of the two oscillators are $\mathbf{P}_{1}=P_{0} e^{-i \omega t} \mathbf{e}_{x}$ and $\mathbf{P}_{2}=P_{0}(\cos \psi_{0} \mathbf{e}_{x}+\sin \psi_{0} \mathbf{e}_{y}) e^{-i(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2})}$. The dipole moment of the whole system is $\mathbf{P}=\mathbf{P}_{1}+\mathbf{P}_{2}$ and the magnetic field in Gaussian units is given by $\mathbf{B}=k^{2} \frac{1}{r} e^{i k r}(\mathbf{e}_{r} \times \mathbf{P})$. Using $\mathbf{e}_{x}=\mathbf{e}_{r}\sin\theta\cos\varphi+\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\cos\theta\cos\varphi-\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}\sin\varphi$, $\mathbf{e}_{y}=\mathbf{e}_{r}\sin\theta\sin\varphi+\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\cos\theta\sin\varphi+\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}\cos\varphi$, and $e^{i\frac{\pi}{2}}=i$, we obtain $\mathbf{e}_{r}\times\mathbf{P}_{1}=P_{0}e^{-i \omega t}(\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}\cos\theta\cos\varphi+\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\sin\varphi)$ and $\mathbf{e}_{r}\times\mathbf{P}_{2}=iP_{0}e^{-i \omega t}[\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}\cos(\varphi-\psi_{0})\cos\theta+\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\sin(\varphi-\psi_{0})]$. Therefore, the magnetic field becomes $\mathbf{B}=\frac{k^{2}P_{0}}{r}\left\{[\sin\varphi+i\sin(\varphi-\psi_{0})]\mathbf{e}_{\theta}+[\cos\varphi+i\cos(\varphi-\psi_{0})]\cos\theta\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}\right\}e^{i(kr-\omega t)}$. (a) The average power per unit solid angle is then calculated as $\frac{d\bar{S}}{d\Omega}=\frac{c}{4\pi}\mathbf{B}\cdot\mathbf{B}^{*}r^{2}=\frac{P_{0}^{2}k^{4}c}{8\pi}\left\{2-\sin^{2}\theta[\cos^{2}\varphi+\cos^{2}(\varphi-\psi_{0})]\right\}$.

raw_question_text: A waveguide is formed by two infinite parallel perfectly conducting planes separated by a distance $a$. The gap between the planes is filled with a gas whose index of refraction is $n$ (this is taken to be frequency independent). A uniform charged wire, which extends infinitely along the $y$ direction (Fig. 5.15), moves in the midplane of the gap with velocity $v>c/n$. It emits Čerenkov radiation. At any fixed point in the gap this reveals itself as time-varying electric and magnetic fields. How does the magnitude of the electric field vary with time at a point in the midplane of the gap?
normalized_question_text: A waveguide is formed by two infinite parallel perfectly conducting planes separated by a distance $a$. The gap between the planes is filled with a gas whose index of refraction is $n$ (taken to be frequency independent). A uniform charged wire, which extends infinitely along the $y$ direction, moves in the midplane of the gap with velocity $v>c/n$. It emits Cerenkov radiation. At any fixed point in the gap this reveals itself as time-varying electric and magnetic fields. How does the magnitude of the electric field vary with time at a point in the midplane of the gap?
reasoning_chain: In vacuum, the electric field at a field point created by a particle of charge $q$ moving with velocity $\mathbf{v}$ is given by $\mathbf{E}=\frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{\alpha\mathbf{R}}{s^3}$, with $\alpha=1-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^2$ and $s=\left[\alpha R^2+\frac{1}{c^2}(\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{R})^2\right]^{1/2}$. In a medium with refractive index $n$, these modify to $\alpha=1-\left(\frac{vn}{c}\right)^2$ and $s=\left[\alpha R^2+\left(\frac{n}{c}\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{R}\right)^2\right]^{1/2}$. For a point $P$ in the midplane where $\mathbf{R}=-vt\,\mathbf{e}_x-y\,\mathbf{e}_y$, by symmetry the contributions along $y$ cancel and the net electric field is along $x$. Writing the contribution as $dE_{x}$ from a charge element and integrating up to the limit determined by the Cerenkov cone yields $E(t)\propto 1/t$, so that $E(t)=\frac{A}{t}$. A Fourier transform then gives $E(\omega)=\frac{A}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\frac{e^{i\omega t}}{t}dt=\frac{Ai}{2}$, demonstrating that each monochromatic component has the same intensity (a white spectrum) and that there is no principal frequency.

raw_question_text: A waveguide is formed by two infinite parallel perfectly conducting planes separated by a distance $a$. The gap between the planes is filled with a gas whose index of refraction is $n$ (this is taken to be frequency independent). Any electromagnetic disturbance (independent of $y$) must be expressible as a superposition of the waveguide modes considered in part (a). What is the mode corresponding to the principal frequency of the Čerenkov spectrum considered in part (b)?
normalized_question_text: A waveguide is formed by two infinite parallel perfectly conducting planes separated by a distance $a$. The gap between the planes is filled with a gas whose index of refraction is $n$ (this is taken to be frequency independent). Any electromagnetic disturbance (independent of $y$) must be expressible as a superposition of the waveguide modes considered in part (a). What is the mode corresponding to the principal frequency of the Čerenkov spectrum considered in part (b)?
reasoning_chain: Let $S$ be the unit vector normal to the upper plane of the wedge which forms the surface of the Cerenkov radiation, with $\mathbf{k}$ in that direction and $|\mathbf{k}|=\frac{n\omega}{c}$. Then we have $k_{x}=\frac{\omega}{c}n\sin\varphi=\frac{\omega}{c}n\left(\frac{c}{nv}\right)=\frac{\omega}{v}$. Since only those frequencies that satisfy $\omega=\frac{c}{n}\sqrt{\left(\frac{\omega}{v}\right)^2+\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2}$ can propagate in the waveguide, solving this relation for $\omega$ leads to $\omega=\frac{m\pi}{a}\left(\frac{n^2}{c^2}-\frac{1}{v^2}\right)^{-1/2}$ for $m=1,2,\ldots$.

raw_question_text: In a recent classic table-top experiment, a monochromatic neutron beam ($\lambda=1.445 \AA$) was split by Bragg reflection at point $A$ of an interferometer into two beams which were recombined (after another reflection) at point $D$. One beam passes through a region of transverse magnetic field of strength $B$ for a distance $l$. Assume that the two paths from $A$ to $D$ are identical except for the region of the field. Find the explicit expressions for the dependence of the intensity at point $D$ on $\mathbf{B}$, $l$ and the neutron wavelength, with the neutron polarized either parallel or anti-parallel to the magnetic field.
normalized_question_text: In a recent classic table-top experiment, a monochromatic neutron beam ($\lambda = 1.445\ \AA$) was split by Bragg reflection at point $A$ of an interferometer into two beams, which were recombined (after another reflection) at point $D$. One beam passes through a region of transverse magnetic field of strength $B$ for a distance $l$. Assume that the two paths from $A$ to $D$ are identical except for the region of the field. Find the explicit expressions for the dependence of the intensity at point $D$ on $\mathbf{B}$, $l$, and the neutron wavelength, with the neutron polarized either parallel or anti-parallel to the magnetic field.
reasoning_chain: This is a problem on spinor interference. Consider a neutron in the beam. There is a magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ in the region where the Schrödinger equation for the (uncharged) neutron is $\left(-\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m}\nabla^{2}-\mu\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}\right)\psi=E\psi$. Supposing $B$ to be constant and uniform, we have $\psi(t_{1})=\exp\left[-i\hat{H}(t_{1}-t_{0})/\hbar\right]\psi(t_{0})$, where $t_{0},t_{1}$ are respectively the instants when the neutron enters and leaves the magnetic field. Write $\psi(t)=\psi(\mathbf{r},t)\psi(\mathbf{s},t)$, where $\psi(\mathbf{r},t)$ and $\psi(\mathbf{s},t)$ are respectively the space and spin parts of $\psi$. Then $\psi(\mathbf{r},t_{1})=\operatorname{ex}\left[-\frac{i}{\hbar}\left(-\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m}\nabla^{2}\right)(t_{1}-t_{0})\right]\psi(\mathbf{r},t_{0})$, which is the same as the wave function of a free particle, and $\psi(\mathbf{s},t_{1})=\exp\left[\frac{i}{\hbar}\mu\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}(t_{1}-t_{0})\right]\psi(s,t_{0})$. The interference arises from the action of $\mathbf{B}$ on the spin wave function. As $\psi(\mathbf{r},t)$ is the wave function of a free particle, we have $t_{1}-t_{0}=l/v=ml/\hbar k$ and $\psi(\mathbf{s},t_{1})=\exp\left[i2\pi\mu ml\lambda\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}/h^{2}\right]\psi(s,t_{0})$, where $k=\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}=mv/\hbar$ is the wave number of the neutron. The intensity of the interference of the two beams at $D$ is then proportional to $\left|\psi_{D}^{(1)}(\mathbf{r},t)\psi_{D}^{(1)}(\mathbf{s},t)+\psi_{D}^{(2)}(\mathbf{r},t)\psi_{D}^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t)\right|^{2}\propto\left|\psi_{D}^{(1)}(\mathbf{s},t)+\psi_{D}^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t)\right|^{2}=\left|\psi^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t_{0})+\psi^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t_{1})\right|^{2}$. As $\exp\left(i\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda}{h^{2}}\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}\right)=\cos\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda B}{h^{2}}+i\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\frac{\mathbf{B}}{B}\sin\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda B}{h^{2}}$, and $\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}=\pm\sigma B$ depending on whether $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$ is parallel or anti-parallel to $B$, we have $\left|\psi^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t_{0})+\psi^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t_{1})\right|^{2}=\left|1+\exp\left(i\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda}{h^{2}}\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}\right)\right|^{2}\left|\psi(\mathbf{s},t_{0})\right|^{2}=\left(1+\cos\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda B}{h^{2}}\right)^{2}+\sin^{2}\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda B}{h^{2}}4\cos^{2}\frac{\pi\mu ml\lambda B}{h^{2}}. \text{ Therefore, the interference intensity at } D\propto\cos^{2}\left(\pi\mu ml\lambda B/h^{2}\right), \text{ where } \mu \text{ is the intrinsic magnetic moment of the neutron } (\mu<0).

raw_question_text: A particle of charge $q$ moving in one dimension is initially bound to a delta function potential at the origin. From time $t=0$ to $t=\tau$ it is exposed to a constant electric field $\varepsilon_{0}$ in the $x$ direction as shown in Fig. 6.15. The object of this problem is to find the probability that for $t>\tau$ the particle will be found in an unbound state with energy between $E_{k}$ and $E_{k}+dE_{k}$. Assume that the electric field may be treated as a perturbation. Write down the perturbation term in the Hamiltonian, $\hat{H}_{1}$, and find the matrix element of $\hat{H}_{1}$ between the initial state and the final state, $(0|\hat{H}_{1}|k)$.
normalized_question_text: A particle of charge $q$ moving in one dimension is initially bound to a delta function potential at the origin. From time $t=0$ to $t=\tau$ it is exposed to a constant electric field $\varepsilon_{0}$ in the $x$ direction as shown in Fig. 6.15. The object of this problem is to find the probability that for $t>\tau$ the particle will be found in an unbound state with energy between $E_{k}$ and $E_{k}+dE_{k}$. Assume that the electric field may be treated as a perturbation. Write down the perturbation term in the Hamiltonian, $\hat{H}_{1}$, and find the matrix element of $\hat{H}_{1}$ between the initial state and the final state, $(0|\hat{H}_{1}|k)$.
reasoning_chain:
Treating $\varepsilon_{0}$ as a perturbation, the perturbation Hamiltonian is
$$\hat{H}_{1}=-q\varepsilon_{0}\,x.$$ 
The matrix element between the initial state and the final state is
$$\langle k|\hat{H}_{1}|0angle=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\psi_{k}^{*}(-q\varepsilon_{0}\,x)\psi\,dx,$$
which becomes
$$- rac{q\varepsilon_{0}}{\sqrt{L}}\sqrt{ rac{mA}{\hbar^{2}}}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}x\exp\Bigl(-ikx-k_{0}|x|\Bigr)dx.$$ 
By writing the $x$ factor as a derivative with respect to $k$,
$$- rac{q\varepsilon_{0}}{\sqrt{L}}\sqrt{ rac{mA}{\hbar^{2}}}i rac{d}{dk}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\exp\Bigl(-ikx-k_{0}|x|\Bigr)dx,$$
and splitting the integral into two regions ($x<0$ and $x>0$), one finds after differentiation
$$\langle k|\hat{H}_{1}|0angle=- rac{q\varepsilon_{0}}{\sqrt{L}}\sqrt{ rac{mA}{\hbar^{2}}} igl(-4ik k_{0} igr) rac{1}{\Bigl[k^{2}+k_{0}^{2}\Bigr]^{2}},$$
which simplifies to
$$\langle k|\hat{H}_{1}|0angle= rac{4i q\varepsilon_{0}}{\sqrt{L}}\Bigl( rac{mA}{\hbar^{2}}\Bigr)^{3/2} rac{k}{\Bigl[k^{2}+\Bigl( rac{mA}{\hbar^{2}}\Bigr)^{2}\Bigr]^{2}}.$$

The object of this problem is to find the probability that for $t>\tau$ the particle will be found in an unbound state with energy between $E_{k}$ and $E_{k}+dE_{k}$. The probability of a transition between an initially occupied state $|I\rangle$ and a final state $|F\rangle$ due to a weak perturbation Hamiltonian $\hat{H}_{1}(t)$ is given by
$$
P_{I\rightarrow F}(t)=\frac{1}{\hbar^{2}}\left|\int_{-\infty}^{t}\langle F|\hat{H}_{1}(t')|I\rangle e^{i\omega_{FI}t'}dt'\right|^{2},
$$
where $\omega_{FI}=\frac{E_{F}-E_{I}}{\hbar}$. Find an expression for the probability $P(E_{k})\,dE_{k}$ that the particle will be in an unbound state with energy between $E_{k}$ and $E_{k}+dE_{k}$ for $t>\tau$.

with

Since
$$ rac{\sin^{2}(\omega_{FI}	au/2)}{(\omega_{FI}/2)^{2}}= rac{\sin^{2}\Bigl\{ rac{\hbar	au}{4m}\Bigl[k^{2}+\Bigl( rac{mA}{\hbar^{2}}\Bigr)^{2}\Bigr]\Bigr\}}{\Bigl\{ rac{\hbar}{4m}\Bigl[k^{2}+\Bigl( rac{mA}{\hbar^{2}}\Bigr)^{2}\Bigr]\Bigr\}^{2}},$$

raw_question_text: A point source $Q$ emits coherent light isotropically at two frequencies $w$ and $w+ \Delta w$ with equal power I joules/sec at each frequency. Two detectors $A$ and $B$ each with a (small) sensitive area $s$, capable of responding to individual photons are located at distances $l_{A}$ and $l_{B}$ from $Q$ as shown in Fig. 8.4. In the following take $\Delta \omega/\omega\ll1$ and assume the experiment is carried out in vacuum. Calculate the individual photon counting rates (photons/sec) at $A$ and $B$ as functions of time. Consider time scales $\gg 1/\omega$.
normalized_question_text: A point source $Q$ emits coherent light isotropically at two frequencies $w$ and $w+ \Delta w$ with equal power $I$ joules/sec at each frequency. Two detectors $A$ and $B$, each with a small sensitive area $s$ and capable of responding to individual photons, are located at distances $l_A$ and $l_B$ from $Q$ as shown in Fig. 8.4. In the following take $\Delta \omega/\omega \ll 1$ and assume the experiment is carried out in vacuum. Calculate the individual photon counting rates (photons/sec) at $A$ and $B$ as functions of time. Consider time scales $\gg 1/\omega$.
reasoning_chain:
The wave function of a photon at $A$ is
$$\psi_{A}(l_{A},t)=C_{1}\left[e^{i\omega\left( rac{l_{A}}{c}-tight)}+e^{i(\omega+\Delta\omega)\left( rac{l_{A}}{c}-tight)}ight],$$
where $C_{1}$ is real and, hence, the probability of finding a photon at $A$ in unit time is
$$ egin{aligned}
P_{A}&=\psi_{A}^{*}\psi_{A}\
&=C_{1}^{2}\left\{2+2\cos\left[\Delta\omega\left( rac{l_{A}}{c}-tight)ight]ight\}\
&=4C_{1}^{2}\cos^{2}\left[ rac{\Delta\omega}{2}\left( rac{l_{A}}{c}-tight)ight].
\end{aligned}$$
If there is only a single frequency, $P_{A}=C_{1}^{2}$. As each photon has energy $\hbar\omega$, the number of photons arriving at $A$ per second is
$$ rac{s}{4\pi l_{A}^{2}}\cdot  rac{I}{\hbar\omega}.$$ 
Hence,
$$C_{1}^{2}= rac{I s}{4\pi l_{A}^{2}\hbar\omega},$$
and
$$P_{A}= rac{I s}{\pi l_{A}^{2}\hbar\omega}\cos^{2}\left[ rac{\Delta\omega}{2}\left( rac{l_{A}}{c}-tight)ight].$$
Similarly we have
$$P_{B}=4C_{2}^{2}\cos^{2}\left[ rac{\Delta\omega}{2}\left( rac{l_{A}}{c}-tight)ight],$$
where
$$C_{2}^{2}= rac{I \varepsilon}{4\pi l_{B}^{2}\hbar\omega}.$$

raw_question_text: A cylindrical container is initially separated by a clamped piston into two compartments of equal volume. The piston is now unclamped and released to move freely without friction. Eventually, due to slight dissipation, it comes to rest in an equilibrium position. Calculate: The ratio of final neon to argon volumes.

The volume ratio is the ratio of molecular numbers, and is also the ratio of initial pressures. Thus
\[

\]
where $n=1 / 4$ is the mole number of the argon gas.

raw_question_text: The elasticity of a rubber band can be described in terms of a onedimensional model of polymer involving $N$ molecules linked together end-to-end. The angle between successive links is equally likely to be $0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$. Find the relationship between the force and the length, without using the condition in (c), i.e., for any possible value of $L$, but $N \gg 1$.
normalized_question_text: The elasticity of a rubber band can be described in terms of a one-dimensional model of polymer involving $N$ molecules linked together end-to-end. The angle between successive links is equally likely to be $0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$. Find the relationship between the force and the length, without using the condition in (c), i.e., for any possible value of $L$, but $N \gg 1$.
reasoning_chain: Consider only one link. When an external force $f$ is exerted, the probability that the angle is $0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$ is proportional to $e^{\alpha}$ or $e^{-\alpha}$ respectively, where $\alpha=f d / k T$. The average length per link is therefore $\bar{l}=d \frac{e^{\alpha}-e^{-\alpha}}{e^{\alpha}+e^{-\alpha}}=d \tanh \alpha$. The overall length of the polymer is then $L=N \bar{l}=N d \tanh (f d / k T)$.

raw_question_text: Consider a one-dimensional chain consisting of $n \\gg 1$ segments as illustrated in the figure. When the length of the chain is $n x$, there are $m=n x / a$ segments parallel to the chain; so the microscopic state number is
\[
\Omega=C_{m}^{n}= rac{n!}{m!(n-m)!} .
\]

We have
\[
 egin{aligned}
S & =k \ln \Omega \
& =k \ln  rac{n!}{\left( rac{x}{a} night)!\left(n- rac{x}{a} night)!} .
\end{aligned}
\]

\[

& =\int_{0}^{\mu} D(E) d E+ rac{\pi^{2}}{6}(k T)^{2} D^{\prime}(\mu)+ rac{7 \pi^{4}}{360}(k T)^{4} D^{\prime \prime \prime}(\mu)+\ldots \
& \approx  rac{8 \pi V(2 m)^{3 / 2}}{3 h^{3}} \mu^{3 / 2}\left[1+ rac{\pi^{2}}{8}\left( rac{k T}{\mu}ight)^{2}ight] .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus, we get
\[
\mu=E_{	ext{F}}\left[1- rac{\pi^{2}}{12}\left( rac{k T}{E_{	ext{F}}}ight)^{2}ight]
\]
where
\[
E_{	ext{F}}=\mu_{0}= rac{1}{2 m}\left( rac{3 h^{3} N}{8 \pi V}ight)^{2 / 3}
\]

raw_question_text:
Consider a one-dimensional solid of length \(L=N a\) made up of \(N\) diatomic molecules, the interatomic spacing within a molecule is \(b\left(b<\frac{a}{2}\right)\). The centers of adjacent molecules are a distance \(a\) apart. We represent the potential energy as a sum of delta functions centered on each atom:
\[
V=-A \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\left[\delta\left(x-n a+\frac{b}{2}\right)+\delta\left(x-n a-\frac{b}{2}\right)\right]
\]
with \(A\) a positive quantity and \(n=0,1,2, \ldots, N-1\). The potential is shown in Fig. 1.18. Expressing the potential as a Fourier series
\[
V=\sum_{q} V_{q} e^{i q x}
\]
find the allowed values of \(q\) and the coefficients \(V_{q}\).
normalized_question_text:
Consider a one-dimensional solid of length \(L = Na\) made up of \(N\) diatomic molecules. The interatomic spacing within a molecule is \(b\left(b < \frac{a}{2}\right)\). The centers of adjacent molecules are a distance \(a\) apart. We represent the potential energy as a sum of delta functions centered on each atom:
\[
V = -A \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[\delta\left(x - na + \frac{b}{2}\right) + \delta\left(x - na - \frac{b}{2}\right)\right]
\]
with \(A\) a positive quantity and \(n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1\). The potential is shown in Fig. 1.18. Expressing the potential as a Fourier series
\[
V = \sum_q V_q e^{iqx}
\]
find the allowed values of \(q\) and the coefficients \(V_q\).
reasoning_chain:
As $V(x)$ has period $a$, in the Fourier series
\[
V(x)=\sum_{q} V_{q} e^{i q x}
\]
we require
\[
e^{i q x}=e^{i q(x+a)}
\]
or
\[
q= rac{2 n \pi}{a}, \quad n=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots
\]
which are the allowed values of $q$. Consider
\[
 egin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{a} V(x) e^{-i q^{\prime} x} d x & =\sum_{q} V_{q} \int_{0}^{a} e^{i  rac{2 \pi}{a}\left(n-n^{\prime}ight) x} d x \
& =\sum_{q} V_{q} a \delta_{q q^{\prime}}=V_{q^{\prime}} a
\end{aligned}
\]
where $q^{\prime}= rac{2 n^{\prime} \pi}{a}$ is a particular $q$. Thus the coefficients are given by
\[
V_{q}= rac{1}{a} \int_{0}^{a} V(x) e^{-i q x} d x
\]

In the neighborhood of $x=n a$,
\[
V(x)=-A\left[\delta\left(x-n a+ rac{b}{2}ight)+\delta\left(x-n a- rac{b}{2}ight)ight],
\]
so that
\[
 egin{aligned}
V_{q} & =- rac{A}{a}\left[\int_{0}^{a} \delta\left(x-n a+ rac{b}{2}ight) e^{-i q x} d x+\int_{0}^{a} \delta\left(x-n a- rac{b}{2}ight) e^{-i q x} d xight] \
& =- rac{A}{a}\left[e^{-i q\left(n a- rac{b}{2}ight)}+e^{-i q\left(n a+ rac{b}{2}ight)}ight] \
& =- rac{A}{a} e^{i q n a} \cdot 2 \cos \left( rac{b q}{2}ight) \
& =- rac{2 A}{a} \cos \left( rac{b q}{2}ight)
\end{aligned}
\]
as $e^{i q n a}=e^{i 2 n^{2} \pi}=1$.

raw_question_text: Consider a one-dimensional periodic potential $U(x)$ viewed as an array of identical potential barriers $V(x)$ of width $a$, centered at the points $x= \\pm n a$ where $n$ is an integer. The barrier $V(x)$, schematically drawn in Fig. 1.19, may be characterized by a transmission coefficient $t(k)$ and a reflection coefficient $r(k)$ for an electron incident on the barrier with energy $E=\\frac{\\hbar^{2} \\kappa^{2}}{2 m}$. Suppose the barriers are weak, i.e., $|t|=1$ and $|r|=0$, find a simple expression for the width of the energy gaps.
normalized_question_text: Consider a one-dimensional periodic potential $U(x)$ viewed as an array of identical potential barriers $V(x)$ of width $a$, centered at the points $x=\pm n a$ where $n$ is an integer. The barrier $V(x)$ may be characterized by a transmission coefficient $t(k)$ and a reflection coefficient $r(k)$ for an electron incident on the barrier with energy $E=\frac{\hbar^{2}\kappa^{2}}{2m}$. Suppose the barriers are weak, i.e., $|t|=1$ and $|r|=0$, find a simple expression for the width of the energy gaps.
reasoning_chain:
Consider an energy gap in the $k$-space with boundaries $k_{1}, k_{2}$ and midpoint at $k_{0}$ and width $2 \epsilon$. It can be seen from Fig. 1.20 and Eq. (2) that
\[
 egin{aligned}
\left|\cos \left(k_{0} a+\deltaight)ight| & =1 \
\left|\cos \left(k_{0} a+\delta+\epsilon aight)ight| & =|t|
\end{aligned}
\]
or
\[
k_{0} a+\delta=n \pi
\]
where $n$ is zero or an integer, and
\[
|\sin (n \pi+\epsilon a)|=\sqrt{1-|t|^{2}}=|r|
\]

For a weak barrier, $|r| ightarrow 0,|t| ightarrow 1,|\delta| ightarrow 0$, and as can be seen from Fig. 1.20, $\epsilon a ightarrow 0$, so that
\[
k_{0}= rac{1}{2}\left(k_{2}+k_{1}ight) \approx  rac{n \pi}{2 a}
\]
and
\[
|\sin (n \pi) \cos (\epsilon a)+\sin (\epsilon a) \cos (n \pi)| \approx \epsilon a=|r|
\]
i.e.,
\[
k_{2}-k_{1}= rac{|r|}{a}
\]

Hence the energy gap has width
\[
\Delta E= rac{\hbar^{2}}{2 m}\left(k_{2}^{2}-k_{1}^{2}ight)= rac{\hbar^{2}}{m} k_{0}\left(k_{2}-k_{1}ight)= rac{n \pi}{2}  rac{\hbar^{2}}{m a^{2}}|r|
\]

raw_question_text: A long thin uniformly magnetized needle of hard steel, length 12 cm, is suspended by a torsionless suspension and makes free, small-angle oscillations about an axis through the center in a horizontal plane in the earth's magnetic field. The period of oscillation is $3-5$ seconds. Estimate the density of magnetization of the steel.
normalized_question_text: A long thin uniformly magnetized needle of hard steel, length 12 cm, is suspended by a torsionless suspension and makes free, small-angle oscillations about an axis through the center in a horizontal plane in the earth's magnetic field. The period of oscillation is $3-5$ seconds. Estimate the density of magnetization of the steel.
reasoning_chain:
As only small-angle oscillations are involved, the earth's magnetic field can be considered uniform.
Let $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ be the magnetic moment of the steel needle. Its potential energy in the earth's magnetic field is $V=-\mu \cdot \mathbf{B}=-\mu B \cos \theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ as shown in Fig. 1.41. The equation of motion is
\[
I \ddot{\theta}=-\frac{d V}{d \theta}=-\mu B \sin \theta \approx-\mu B \theta
\]
$I$ being the moment of inertia of the needle, for small angle oscillations. The angular frequency of oscillation is given by
\[
\omega^{2}=\frac{\mu B}{I}
\]

Let $l, V, \rho, \mathbf{M}$ be respectively the length, volume, density and magnetization of the steel needle. Then
\[
I=\frac{\rho V l^{2}}{12}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}=V \mathbf{M}
\]

Light from the quasar is gravitationally bent by the galaxy before reaching us. The angle $\phi$, as measured in the rest frame of the galaxy, is given by $\phi=4 M / r$, where $M$ is the mass of the galaxy and $r$ the impact parameter of the light ray. Thus, as seen by us, the quasar is a ring whose diameter subtends an angle $2 \theta$. The universe has a geometry described by the metric
\[
d s^{2}=-d t^{2}+a^{2}(t)\left(d \rho^{2}+\rho^{2} d \theta^{2}+\rho^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta d \phi^{2}\right)
\]

raw_question_text: A photomultiplier tube is to be used to detect light pulses each of which consists of a small but fixed number of photons. The average photoelectric efficiency is $10 \%$. That is, a photon has a $10 \%$ probability of causing the emission of a detectable photoelectron. Assume the photomultiplier gain is $10^{6}$ and that the output current as a function of time can be approximated as shown in Fig. 3.6. What is $N$, the average number of photons in each pulse?
normalized_question_text: A photomultiplier tube is to be used to detect light pulses each of which consists of a small but fixed number of photons. The average photoelectric efficiency is $10 \%$. That is, a photon has a $10 \%$ probability of causing the emission of a detectable photoelectron. Assume the photomultiplier gain is $10^{6}$ and that the output current as a function of time can be approximated as shown in Fig. 3.6. What is $N$, the average number of photons in each pulse?
reasoning_chain:
The total quantity of charge carried by one pulse of current is
\[
Q=\int I d t
\]
which is the area of the triangle in Fig. 3.6. Thus
\[
Q= rac{1}{2} 	imes 20 	imes 10^{-9} 	imes 80 	imes 10^{-6}=8 	imes 10^{-13} \mathrm{C}
\]
and the number of electrons carried by one pulse is
\[
n= rac{Q}{e}= rac{8 	imes 10^{-13}}{1.6 	imes 10^{-19}}=5 	imes 10^{6} .
\]

Then the number of photoelectrons emitted per light pulse is
\[
n^{\prime}=n / 10^{6}=5
\]
and hence the number of photons in one light pulse is
\[
N=n^{\prime} / 0.1=50 .
\]

raw_question_text: The simplest model for low-lying states of nuclei with $N$ and $Z$ between 20 and 28 involves only $f_{7/2}$ nucleons. Using this model predict the magnetic dipole moments of ${ }_{20}^{41} \text{Ca}_{21}$ and ${ }_{21}^{41} \text{Sc}_{20}$. Estimate crudely the electric quadrupole moments for these two cases as well.
normalized_question_text: The simplest model for low-lying states of nuclei with $N$ and $Z$ between 20 and 28 involves only $f_{7/2}$ nucleons. Using this model predict the magnetic dipole moments of ${ }_{20}^{41} \text{Ca}_{21}$ and ${ }_{21}^{41} \text{Sc}_{20}$. Estimate crudely the electric quadrupole moments for these two cases as well.
reasoning_chain:
${ }^{41} \text{Ca}$ has a neutron and ${ }^{41} \text{Sc}$ has a proton outside closed shells in state $1 f_{7 / 2}$. As closed shells do not contribute to the nuclear magnetic moment, the latter is determined by the extra-shell nucleons. The nuclear magnetic moment is given by
$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}=g \mathbf{j} \mu_{N},
$$
where $\mathbf{j}$ is the total angular momentum, $\mu_{N}$ is the nuclear magneton. For a single nucleon in a central field, the $g$-factor is (Problem 2061)
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
g=\frac{(2 j-1) g_{l}+g_{s}}{2 j} & \text { for } j=l+\frac{1}{2}, 
g=\frac{(2 j+3) g_{l}-g_{s}}{2(j+1)} & \text { for } j=l-\frac{1}{2} .
\end{array}
$$

For neutron, $g_{l}=0, g_{s}=g_{n}=-\frac{1.91}{\frac{1}{2}}=-3.82$. As $l=3$ and $j=\frac{7}{2}=3+\frac{1}{2}$, we have for ${ }^{41} \text{Ca}$
$$
\mu\left({ }^{41} C a\right)=-\frac{3.82}{2 j} \times j \mu_{N}=-1.91 \mu_{N} .
$$

For proton, $g_{l}=1, g_{s}=g_{p}=\frac{2.79}{1 / 2}=5.58$. As $j=\frac{7}{2}=3+\frac{1}{2}$, we have for ${ }^{41} \text{Sc}$
$$
\mu\left({ }^{41} S c\right)=\frac{(7-1)+5.58}{7} \times \frac{7}{2} \mu_{N}=5.79 \mu_{N} .
$$

Note that these values are only in rough agreement with the given experimental values.

The electric quadrupole moment of ${ }^{41} \text{Sc}$, which has a single proton outside closed shells, is given by
$$
Q\left({ }^{41} S c\right)=-e^{2}\left\langle r^{2}\right\rangle \frac{2 j-1}{2(j+1)}=-\left\langle r^{2}\right\rangle \frac{2 j-1}{2(j+1)},
$$
where $\left\langle r^{2}\right\rangle$ is the mean-square distance from the center and the proton charge is taken to be one. For an order-of-magnitude estimate take $\left\langle r^{2}\right\rangle=$ $\left(1.2 \times A^{1 / 3}\right)^{2} \mathrm{fm}^{2}$. Then
$$
Q\left({ }^{41} S c\right)=-\frac{6}{9} \times\left(1.2 \times 41^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)^{2}=-1.14 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$
${ }^{41} \text{Ca}$ has a neutron outside the full shells. Its electric quadrupole moment is caused by the polarization of the neutron relative to the nucleus center and is
$$
Q\left({ }^{41} C a\right) \approx \frac{Z}{(A-1)^{2}}\left|Q\left({ }^{41} S c\right)\right|=1.43 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$

reasoning_chain:
As shown in Fig. 2.15 the ground state of ${ }^{42} \text{Ca}$ nucleus is $0^{+}$. The two last neutrons, which are in $f_{7 / 2}$ can be coupled to form levels of $J=$ $7,6,5 \ldots, 0$ and positive parity. Taking into account the antisymmetry for identical particles, the possible levels are those with $J=6,4,2,0$. (We require $L+S=$ even, see Problem 2054. As $S=0, J=$ even.)
The magnetic dipole moment $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ of a two-nucleon system is given by
$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}=g \mathbf{J} \mu_{N}=\left(g_{1} \mathbf{j}_{1}+g_{2} \mathbf{j}_{2}\right) \mu_{N}
$$
with $\mathbf{J}=\mathbf{j}_{1}+\mathbf{j}_{2}$. As
$$
\begin{aligned}
g \mathbf{J}^{2} & =g_{1} \mathbf{j}_{1} \cdot \mathbf{J}+g_{2} \mathbf{j}_{2} \cdot \mathbf{J}, 
\mathbf{j}_{1} \cdot \mathbf{J} & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathbf{J}^{2}+\mathbf{j}_{1}^{2}-\mathbf{j}_{2}^{2}\right), 
\mathbf{j}_{2} \cdot \mathbf{J} & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathbf{J}^{2}+\mathbf{j}_{2}^{2}-\mathbf{j}_{1}^{2}\right),
\end{aligned}
$$
we have
$$
g \mathbf{J}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right) \mathbf{J}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left(g_{1}-g_{2}\right)\left(\mathbf{j}_{1}^{2}-\mathbf{j}_{2}^{2}\right) .
$$
or
$$
g=\frac{1}{2}\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(g_{1}-g_{2}\right) \frac{j_{1}\left(j_{1}+1\right)-j_{2}\left(j_{2}+1\right)}{J(J+1)} .
$$

For ${ }^{42} \text{Ca}$, the two nucleons outside full shells each has $j=7 / 2$. As
$$
g_{1}=g_{2}=\frac{-3.82}{j_{1}}, \quad j_{1}=\frac{7}{2},
$$
we have $\mu\left({ }^{42} C a\right)=g_{1} J \mu_{N}=-1.09 J \mu_{N}$ with $J=0,2,4,6$.
The ground-state quadrupole moment of ${ }^{42} \text{Ca}$ is $Q=0$. One can get the excited state quadrupole moment using the reduced transition rate for $\gamma$-transition
$$
B\left(E 2,2^{+} \rightarrow 0^{+}\right)=\frac{e^{2} Q_{0}^{2}}{16 \pi}
$$
where $Q_{0}$ is the intrinsic electric quadrupole moment. The first excited state $2^{+}$of ${ }^{42} \text{Ca}$ has excitation energy 1.524 MeV and
$$
B\left(E 2: 2^{+} \rightarrow 0^{+}\right)=81.5 e^{2} \mathrm{fm}^{4},
$$
or
$$
Q_{0}=\sqrt{16 \pi \times 81.5}=64 \mathrm{fm}^{2} .
$$

For other states the quadrupole moments are given by
$$
Q=\frac{K^{2}-J(J+1)}{(J+1)(2 J+3)} Q_{0}=-\frac{J(J+1) Q_{0}}{(J+1)(2 J+3)}=\frac{-J}{2 J+3} Q_{0}
$$
as $K=0$. Thus $Q=18.3 \mathrm{fm}^{2}$ for $J=2,23.3 \mathrm{fm}^{2}$ for $J=4$, and $25.6 \mathrm{fm}^{2}$ for $J=6$.

raw_question_text: What is the best neutron magic number between those for $^{40} \text{Ca}$ and $^{208} \text{Pb}$ ?
normalized_question_text: What is the best neutron magic number between those for $^{40} \text{Ca}$ and $^{208} \text{Pb}$?
reasoning_chain: The neutron magic numbers between $^{40}\text{Ca}$ and $^{208}\text{Pb}$ are 28,50 and 82. For nuclei of $N=Z$, at the neutron magic number $N=28$ the separation energies are about 13 MeV. At neutron number $N=50$, the separation energies are also about 13 MeV. At $\text{N}=82$, the separation energies are about 12 MeV. However, for heavy nuclei, there are more neutrons than protons, so $A<2N$. On account of this, for the nuclei of magic numbers 50 and 82, the separation energies are somewhat less than those given above. At the magic number 28 the separation energy is highest, and so this is the best neutron magic number.

raw_question_text:
Consider the following energy level structure (Fig. 2.30): The ground states form an isotriplet as do the excited states (all states have a spin-parity of $0^{+}$). The ground state of $_{21}^{42} \text{Sc}$ can $\beta$-decay to the ground state of $_{20}^{42} \text{Ca}$ with a kinetic end-point energy of 5.4 MeV (transition II in Fig. 2.30). Consider isospin mixing due to electromagnetic interactions. In general 
 H_{E M}=H_{0}+H_{1}+H_{2}, 
 where the subscripts refer to the isospin tensor rank of each term. Write the branching ratio $\frac{\text{Γ}_{I}}{\text{Γ}_{II}}$ in terms of the reduced matrix elements of each part of $H_{E M}$ which mixes the states.
normalized_question_text:
Consider the following energy level structure (Fig. 2.30): The ground states form an isotriplet as do the excited states (all states have a spin-parity of $0^{+}$). The ground state of $_{21}^{42} \text{Sc}$ can $\beta$-decay to the ground state of $_{20}^{42} \text{Ca}$ with a kinetic end-point energy of 5.4 MeV (transition II in Fig. 2.30). Consider isospin mixing due to electromagnetic interactions. In general,
$H_{EM}=H_{0}+H_{1}+H_{2}$,
where the subscripts refer to the isospin tensor rank of each term. Write the branching ratio $\frac{\Gamma_{I}}{\Gamma_{II}}$ in terms of the reduced matrix elements of each part of $H_{EM}$ which mixes the states.
reasoning_chain:
We make use of the perturbation theory. Let the ground and excited states of $^{42}\text{Ca}$ be $|1\rangle$ and $|2\rangle$ respectively. Because of the effect of $H_{EM}$, the states become mixed. Let the mixed states be $|1\rangle^{\prime}$ and $|2\rangle^{\prime}$, noting that the mixing due to $H_{EM}$ is very small. We have
$$\begin{aligned}
H^{0}|1\rangle & =E_{1}|1\rangle, 
H^{0}|2\rangle & =E_{2}|2\rangle,
\end{aligned}$$
where $E_{1}$ and $E_{2}$ are the energies of the two states $(E_{1} \approx E_{0}, E_{2}-E_{1}=1.8 \text{ MeV})$.

Consider
$$ H=H^{0}+H_{EM}, $$
where $H_{EM}=H_{0}+H_{1}+H_{2}$. As the index refers to isospin tensor rank, we write $H_{0}, H_{1}, H_{2}$ as $P_{0,0}, P_{1,0}, P_{2,0}$ and define
$$ \langle J_{1} m_{1} | P_{\mu 
u} | J_{2} m_{2} \rangle = C_{\mu 
u J_{2} m_{2}}^{J_{1} m_{1}} \langle J_{1} | P_{\mu 
u} | J_{2} \rangle. $$
Then
$$\begin{aligned}
H_{EM}= & P_{0,0}+P_{1,0}+P_{2,0} 
\langle 1| H_{EM}|2\rangle= & \langle \alpha, 1,-1| (P_{0,0}+P_{1,0}+P_{2,0}) |\alpha^{\prime}, 1,-1\rangle 
= & \left( \langle \alpha, 1| P_{0} |\alpha^{\prime}, 1\rangle - \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} \langle \alpha, 1| P_{1} |\alpha^{\prime}, 1\rangle \right) 
& + \sqrt{\frac{1}{10}} \langle \alpha, 1| P_{2} |\alpha^{\prime}, 1\rangle 
\langle 1| H_{EM}|1\rangle & =\langle 2| H_{EM}|2\rangle=\langle \alpha, 1,-1| (P_{0,0}+P_{1,0}+P_{2,0}) |\alpha, 1,-1\rangle 
= & \langle \alpha, 1| P_{0} |\alpha, 1\rangle - \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} \langle \alpha, 1| P_{1} |\alpha, 1\rangle + \sqrt{\frac{1}{10}} \langle \alpha, 1| P_{2} |\alpha, 1\rangle.\end{aligned}$$
In the above equations, $\alpha$ and $\alpha^{\prime}$ denote the quantum numbers of $|1\rangle$ and $|2\rangle$ other than the isospin, and $\langle \alpha, 1| P |\alpha, 1\rangle$ denote the relevant part of the reduced matrix element. Thus
$$
\frac{\Gamma_{I}}{\Gamma_{II}}=\frac{E_{1}^{5} \langle M_{1} \rangle^{2}}{E_{2}^{5} \langle M_{2} \rangle^{2}}=\frac{(5.4-1.8-\langle 2| H_{EM}|2\rangle)^{5}}{(5.4-\langle 1| H_{EM}|1\rangle)^{5}} \frac{\langle 1| H_{EM}|2\rangle^{2}}{(E_{2}-E_{1})^{2}}.
$$

If energy level corrections can be ignored, then $\langle 1| H_{EM}|1\rangle \ll E_{1}, E_{2}$, and
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Gamma_{I}}{\Gamma_{II}} & \approx \frac{E_{10}^{5}}{E_{20}^{5} (E_{2}-E_{1})^{2}} \langle \langle 1| H_{EM} |2\rangle \rangle 
& =\frac{(5.4-1.8)^{5}}{5.4^{5} \times 1.8^{2}} \left( \langle 1| P_{0} |2\rangle - \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} \langle 1| P_{1} |2\rangle + \sqrt{\frac{1}{10}} \langle 1| P_{2} |2\rangle \right)^{2}.
\end{aligned}$$
If we ignore the contribution of $H_{2}$ and assume $\langle 1| P_{0} |2\rangle=0$, then the isoscalar $H$ does not mix the two isospin states and we have
$$
\frac{\Gamma_{I}}{\Gamma_{II}}=\frac{E_{10}^{5}}{E_{20}^{5} (E_{2}-E_{1})^{2}}|\langle \alpha, 1| P_{1} |\alpha^{\prime}, 1\rangle|.
$$

raw_question_text: In experiments on the reaction $^{21}\text{Ne}(\text{d, }^{3}\text{He}){}^{20}\text{F}$ with 26 MeV deuterons, many states in $^{20}\text{F}$ are excited. The angular distributions are characteristic of the direct reaction mechanism and therefore are easily sorted into those for which the angular momentum of the transferred proton is $l_{p}=0$ or 1 or 2. The lowest energy levels of $^{21}\text{Ne}$ and the known negative‐parity states of $^{20}\text{F}$ below 4 MeV are as shown in Fig. 2.41 (the many positive‐parity excited states of $^{20}\text{F}$ are omitted). The relative $S\text{(}J^{\text{π}}\text{)}$ observed in the (d, $^{3}\text{He}$) reaction are approximately: $S\text{(}1^{-}\text{)}=0.84$, $S\text{(}2_{1}^{-}\text{)}=0.78$, $S\text{(}2_{2}^{-}\text{)}=0.79$, $S\text{(}3^{-}\text{)}=0.00$. In the limit of weak coupling; i.e., with no residual interaction between the hole and the particles, what would be the (relative) energies of the 4 negative‐parity states?
normalized_question_text: In experiments on the reaction $^{21}\text{Ne}(\text{d, }^{3}\text{He}){}^{20}\text{F}$ with 26 MeV deuterons, many states in $^{20}\text{F}$ are excited. The angular distributions are characteristic of the direct reaction mechanism and therefore are easily sorted into those for which the angular momentum of the transferred proton is $l_{p}=0$ or 1 or 2. The lowest energy levels of $^{21}\text{Ne}$ and the known negative‐parity states of $^{20}\text{F}$ below 4 MeV are as shown in Fig. 2.41 (the many positive‐parity excited states of $^{20}\text{F}$ are omitted). The relative $S\text{(}J^{\text{π}}\text{)}$ observed in the (d, $^{3}\text{He}$) reaction are approximately: $S\text{(}1^{-}\text{)}=0.84$, $S\text{(}2_{1}^{-}\text{)}=0.78$, $S\text{(}2_{2}^{-}\text{)}=0.79$, $S\text{(}3^{-}\text{)}=0.00$. In the limit of weak coupling; i.e., with no residual interaction between the hole and the particles, what would be the (relative) energies of the 4 negative‐parity states?
reasoning_chain: We have $H=H_{p}+H_{h}+V_{p h}$, where $H_{p}$ and $H_{h}$ are respectively the Hamiltonian of the nuclear center and the hole, and $V_{p h}$ is the potential due to the interaction of the hole and the nuclear center. In the limit of weak coupling, $V_{p h}=0$. Then for the four negative-parity states we have $E_{3^{-}}=E_{2_{1}^{-}}, E_{2_{2}^{-}}=E_{1^{-}}$, as shown in Fig. 2.42, with values $E_{3^{-}}=E_{2_{1}^{-}}=1230 \text{keV}, E_{2_{2}^{-}}=E_{1^{-}}=890 \text{keV}$.

raw_question_text: Inelastic neutrino scattering in the quark model. Consider the scattering of neutrinos on free, massless quarks. We will simplify things and discuss only strangeness-conserving reactions, i.e. transitions only between the $u$ and $d$ quarks. The experimental value is $\sigma^{\bar{u} N} / \sigma^{u N} = 0.37 \pm 0.02$. What does this value tell you about the quark/antiquark structure of the nucleon?
normalized_question_text: Inelastic neutrino scattering in the quark model. Consider the scattering of neutrinos on free, massless quarks. We will simplify things and discuss only strangeness-conserving reactions, i.e. transitions only between the $u$ and $d$ quarks. The experimental value is $\sigma^{\bar{u} N} / \sigma^{u N} = 0.37 \pm 0.02$. What does this value tell you about the quark/antiquark structure of the nucleon?
reasoning_chain:
The experimental value $\sigma\left(\bar{u} N\right) / \sigma\left(u N\right)=0.37 \pm 0.02$ is approximately the same as the theoretical value $1 / 3$. This means that nucleons consist mainly of quarks, any antiquarks present would be very small in proportion. Let the ratio of antiquark to quark in a nucleon be $\alpha$, then
$\frac{\sigma\left(\bar{u} N\right)}{\sigma\left(u N\right)}=\frac{3 \sigma\left(\bar{u} u\right)+3 \alpha \sigma\left(\bar{u} \bar{d}\right)}{3 \sigma\left(u d\right)+3 \alpha \sigma\left(u \bar{u}\right)}=\frac{3 \times \frac{1}{3}+3 \alpha \times 1}{3 \times 1+3 \alpha \times \frac{1}{3}}=\frac{1+3 \alpha}{3+\alpha}=0.37$
giving
$\frac{1+3 \alpha}{8}=\frac{0.37}{2.63}$
or
$\alpha=4 \times 10^{-2}$

raw_question_text: Consider a $2-\text{cm}$ thick plastic scintillator directly coupled to the surface of a photomultiplier with a gain of $10^{6}$. A $10-\text{GeV}$ particle beam is incident on the scintillator as shown in Fig. 4.8(a). If the beam particle is a muon, estimate the charge collected at the anode of the photomultiplier.
normalized_question_text: Consider a $2-\text{cm}$ thick plastic scintillator directly coupled to the surface of a photomultiplier with a gain of $10^{6}$. A $10-\text{GeV}$ particle beam is incident on the scintillator as shown in Fig. 4.8(a). If the beam particle is a muon, estimate the charge collected at the anode of the photomultiplier.
reasoning_chain: From its ionization loss curve, we see that a muon of energy 10 GeV will lose 4 MeV in a plastic scintillator of length 2 cm . Roughly, in a plastic scintillator, producing one photon requires 100 eV of energy. This amount of energy will produce $N_{\text{ph}} \approx 4 \times 10^{4}$ photons in the scintillator. Suppose about $50 %$ of the photons make it to the photomultiplier tube and about $10 %$ of these produce photoelectrons off the cathode. Then the number of photoelectrons emitted is $N_{\text{pe}}=2 \times 10^{3}$. With a gain of $10^{6}$, the charge collected at the anode of the photomultiplier is $Q=2 \times 10^{9} e=3.2 \times 10^{-10} \text{C}$
